commentBody: Felt it was a bit hard for a Monday
There's a mistake in the app. RAYCHARLES should be highlighted. Not RUMBA.

HIP, HIP, HURRUMBA!! 😏

The highlighting on the app is on 49 down, instead of 49 across, so now I have a new cheer to cheer! Whoohoo, indeed!

Fun puzzle! I liked it! Felt a little more engaging to me than a lot of Mondays.

Because the first theme clue was musical, I decided to put the revealer in right away and shows I'm so excited...  Alas, 'twas not to be.
When I spotted my error in the form of RAMCHARLES I must admit I felt a bit sheepish. But MALTA seemed a plausible place for the Allies to meet
You had me at RAYCHARLES. He doesn’t need highlighting but should appear more often.  His name puts me in a good mood.
FYI, the text of the clue for 62A has the correct cross-references, but for me it highlights 49D instead of 49A.
Perfect Monday for me. Nice theme and the cluing felt right. Streak now at 2 after that brutal SW on Saturday.

Brad, this was a jolly good Monday. 
Peter, that is one of the cutest pictures I’ve seen leading the column!
Glad I didn’t solve in pen today, I had GETIT before GETME, SAMBA and MAMBO before RUMBA, MALTA before YALTA (ok I didn’t read the clue first) and TAB before TAP (although I’ve never seen a keg with a pull tab).
Agree it’s always a pleasure to see a callout for the Genius of Soul.
Given 24-across and today’s picture at the top of the column, I propose a caption: “What’s in your owlet?”

Has anyone ever said "I must gird my loins!" without giggling?  

Any why doesn't anyone ever UNgird their loins?  I've never heard anyone say "Boy, my loins sure feel a lot better now that I've ungirded all that girding.  They have a chance to breathe now. Both of them."  (Question for later, does anyone have a loin surgically removed so they can only gird a loin?)

I don't think I've ever girded my loins, unless "gird" means something I'm unaware of.  In that case I might have.

Maybe that's the key to those of you not being challenged by the puzzle.  Instead of girding your loins for the challenge, just give them a light truss.

Obviously if you've girded your loins all good and proper, but the the puzzle is a pushover, then you're just standing there looking (and probably feeling) silly with those well-girded loins.

That's all I have to say about girding loins.

Monday funday. day appropriate and some fun fill and clueing. Thx Brad Wiegmann!

I have friends in Yosemite tonight and heavy snow is predicted; should be beautiful, but crossing fingers they have easy arrival and departure.

Point of order/nit/question: is a TRIathlon a race, or an event encompassing three races? asking for a friend.

Does anyone else know where the i in team can be found? it's NSFW and requires a visual, but i always think of it whenever i encounter that phrase. 

39A felt personal. On Thursday we closed on the sale of our beach home. it was a modest apartment, but it was home for 38 summers, and saying goodbye is really hard. 

not sure how i feel about BIT and ATAD; maybe there should have been an "A" in the clue for BIT to keep it symmetrical?

I'm out. seeya later, or more likely in the am.
The emus must not be familiar with Ray Charles; they are blocking my comment using the song title in the clue.

My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Famous figure known for off-the-cuff performances?  (7)
2. Oil bigwig  (3)(7)
3. Figures in a speed trap?  (5)
4. Dark green  (4)
5. Line of latitude?  (3)(5)(2)


HOUDINI
ART CURATOR
NARCS
KALE
I'LL ALLOW IT

Neat coincidence with today's photo - just before doing the puzzle this morning, I started reading Inside Animal Hearts and Minds, by Belinda Recio. In the intro, she recommends other recent animal books, all of which (except those I've already read) I added to my queue, one of which is, The Hidden Lives of Owls, by Leigh Calvez.
I'm a bit surprised that the puzzle editors and testers of were okay with the 24A clue. This is the NEW YORK Times (and the clue is also inappropriate in much of the rest of the world).
Ha, since I had all of the shaded filled and none of 62A, and racing for a Monday PB, I decided HIPHIPHURRU must be a thing.
Hip hip hurrum
I got slowed down because I kept trying to parse 24A as HIPPO ____S and because I thought the YALTA Conference was actually the mALTA Conference...

Fun, good to see that dog again. Asta pops up a few times a year, reminding me each time that I've tried to enjoy those Nick and Nora movies of the 30s and 40s starring William Powell and Myrna Loy (and their cute dog), but can never get into their wisecracking and the contrived plots.  So each time Asta appears, I go to Netflix or wherever and give one of them a go -- and it's always no go. But I have no egrets. 

I wonder -- Lewis might know -- if this grid is aerated with an unusually high number of entries beginning "A" -- Asta, Aesop, apple tart, amen, a tad, Abdul, as we speak, akita, Ana, alley, and Amos ate in -- a basket from KFC and a beer with his wife, Tess (both of them, as a result of this diet, were pleasingly plump) after which they watched on their old RCA three Cheers episodes on the same channel where they sometimes reran old football games from the 70s, with Howard Cosell announcing. Amos is now offering Tess a choice between a German torte or an apple tart with vanilla ice cream. He sure is an asset, that Amos!
"The Thin Man terrier" should be a World Heritage crossword clue by now. I remember my grandma (who loved complaining about the crossword) pointing out what an old standby it was many decades ago.
The incorrect clue is highlighted in the app. It should be 49 across instead of 49 down. Hip hip hurray instead of hip hip hurrum.

Very nice Monday, and quickly taken care of.

Thanks, Brad!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
If a Monday puzzle could have a jaunty, self-referential avatar this one might have William Powell from The Thin Man films.

This grid had its fedora perfectly angled for a Monday.

On a more confused note, can someone explain why Barry thinks HIPPOCKETS is inappropriate?

I'm afraid his own explanation only confused me further.
Hip hip hurrumba

What is happening with the MIDI crossword puzzle? Suddenly it says this page no longer exits when you go to Games and click on 
MIDI ?? Then you try later - and it works???
I haven’t touched anything on my devices .
Seems to be happening to others as well as per comments on line

Yesterday, immediately after completing a puzzle from the archives with [Like some owls] solving to horned, Cornell Lab sent this to my email: https://www.allaboutbirds.org/cams/wildflower-great-horned-owls/ The first video is live, and if you scroll down a little there are some highlights. When I saw today's column photo, I was hoping for a horned owl, but an image search revealed that it's an unknown-to-me mountain scops owl. In case anyone was wondering.
Very nice.
The theme was really clever and fun today.
I think HIP HIP HURRUMBA might just catch on. Will surely need to try it at our next celebration.
The Afghan langauge is PASHTO. The ethnic group who soak it are the Pashtun...
Hip Hip Hurrum!

Congratulations to Erik Agard, winner of the 2026 American Crossword Puzzle Tournament! If you've never seen an ACPT finals, where the three solvers with the highest scores, each in front of a huge blank puzzle, compete in front of the audience to see who correctly fills in the puzzle the fastest -- it's worth a look! And here you can see it, from yesterday: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bHEXRaaNTqM .
Hip Hip Hurrum!! I solved it! ;)
Spelling error 60 across ??

This made for an excellent puzzle for new solvers.  There were plenty of gimmes for footholds, balanced by possible no-knows (THEA, ASTA, PASHTO, YALTA, i.e.) where the solver can learn that even unknown answers can be gotten.  

It also has an easy-to-understand theme, and many new solvers come into Crosslandia not knowing that puzzles can have themes.

My brain liked playing Guess The Revealer, even though unsuccessful.  I liked the zingy theme answers, as well as the local beauty: AS WE SPEAK, APPLE TART, PLUMP, AKITA.  

And wordnerd me loved seeing five palindromes in the grid.

Just a lovely Monday that you made, Brad.  It always feels good to travel through a patch of quality – thank you!

The adorable owlet in today's column was famous for his incredible escapes from cages, straight-jackets and towels.

Harry Hoodini.

@Peter Blair

I can't understand why anyone would complain about a Wordplay columnist referencing their own experience.  What else is a columnist going to do???

And I vibed with your experience.  I only occasionally solve in ink these days, doing the majority of my "work" on-line.  But I did, and still do, enjoy solving with a pen.  And it's a joy when the finished puzzle has no smears or errors.

However, like you, that's a rarity.  None of us is perfect.  But sometimes we can make it *look* like we didn't make any errors.
HIP HIP HURRAY for the letter "I"!

But why is the ink-solve so thrilling to us?
Who are we trying to impress?  That person next to us on the subway???
If the cosmos is just, somewhere there is a record being kept of our special feat!

A fun one indeed.  I missed RAY as a themer so did not see the reveal coming which added a brief moment of puzzlement.  

I'll weigh in on the wallet in the back (hip) pocket theme:  I often work outside in sandy environments and the stuff gets everywhere to the point that my credit cards would become worn and not from use.  

So the cards, ID, etc. are now wrapped in a rubber band and live in the breast pocket.  

Cash, you ask?  Who carries cash? (Actually, the singles for the gas attendants are in an old pill bottle in the truck.)
Fun and easy-peasy Monday puzzle…solved in under average time. Happy Monday and have a great week all!

28A. ...maybe not, but there is a ME. Just sayin'
Ooh, did I get stymied in the NW corner, or what?
Enjoyed the puzzle!

Ah, Peter, a man after my own heart. I also do the puzzle in ink, and agree that there is no better letter than "I," especially if you solve puzzles using lower case. Lots of letters can become others with a fillip here or there.
STP is not a motor oil.

I wonder if any others of our puzzle crowd know how to TAT?  (I don't use shuttles, instead "needle TATting" with a blunt 5-inch needle.  It's a portable "hand-work" project I carry to meetings of Stitchers here; I no longer do hand quilting. Arthritis.)  Recent debate:  would TSA confiscate my needle if I had it in my purse?  (We have 2 flights and a layover on the 19th.)  '

Oh, the puzzle!  (It had TAT in it, so it's Brad's fault I got redirected.)  
Nice little puzzle!  I rather enjoyed seeing "partials" like LUMP and HIPPO and ARLES.  
ADHERE before COHERE...can someone explain the difference between those terms?
I'll check back later for answers to my two queries!

I've been advised while in NYC to put my wallet somewhere other than in my hip pocket because pickpockets are, presumably, a problem.  As for the clue, it doesn't suggests that hip pockets are the only places to put a wallet, just one of the places. Some guys I know put their wallets in their man purses.

Fun Monday puzzle and a clever theme. Was almost done before I caught on and that's always a nice touch. And... an unusual puzzle find today. A Sunday from March 11, 2018 by Matthew Sewell with the title: "If found, call....". Some theme clues and answers: 86a - "Last seen chasing down clues. If found call____(see 24-across)" NICKANDNORA And, 24a - eASTAsia 28a - "Last seen riding in a basket. If found, call___(see 106-across" DOROTHYGALE 106a - goTOTOwn 64a - "Last seen with a red-haired girl. If found call (see 119-across" 119a - xSANDYs Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/11/2018&g=86&d=A ...
Disgrace. Those were the days.
Hip, Hip, Hurray! A quick Monday solve. Thanks, Brad!
Make an 'S' out of an 'I'? Child's play : $

Fun easy breezy monday :-) when i started solving crosswords the beginning of the week would get a little sticky for me, now that’s no longer the case and there’s something so delightful about whizzing quickly through a puzzle as a newer solver. my brain seems to give myself a pat on my own back for a job well done.
On my app, 49 down is highlighted as opposed to 49 across. No big deal but it makes it look like RUM is the connection, not RAY.

Nice puzzle to start the week. The yellow highlight at square 49 going down instead of across caused ATAD of confusion, easily overcome. On a side note, as the proud parent of a University of Miami graduate,  I must state that every season is HURRICANESEASON.

I completed the yesterday's puzzle on my phone, but it seems not to have registered with my account, even though it's showing a gold star in my app. Any way my streak can be saved? I had over 250 days going... Really don't want to throw that away! :(

For the clue "Tehre are two in tihs clue", the answer TYPOS is a mistake. For, "Tehre" and "tihs" were deliberately placed in the clue so cannot be considered errors. (As a result, the clue has only one typo, not two.)
As a member of the family, “Take the ‘A’ Train” was written by Billy Strayhorn and became the hit opener for Duke Ellington. He was composer for the band.

Well, when your revealer is THREECHEERS and you have four themers ...

Okay, it's Monday. The puzzle was fine, though a few of the entries seemed a bit meaty for the day. Done in about 6 minutes, which for me is just about as fast as I can type without having to edit repeatedly.
Hiphop hippoc hurricarumba!
I might have filled in the answer a hundred times, but I've never actually had a Mai Tai. Are they good?
As a longtime pen solver, I have another tip:  when you’re not sure if it’s i before e just put 2 “i”s in.  Voila!

I've never solved a crossword in pen, but because I always solve digitally, I use the digital equivalent of pen on Mondays, Minis, and (now) Midis. The other days, I mark with the digital pencil on my first trip through the puzzle (which, other than on the aforementioned MMM puzzles, always starts with the downs).

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ something there is that ye shall find
in tihs the slightest pile of time
d/ the line that pairs the chain that rhymes
and the hurricane
a/ in the typos

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from December 31 (!), 2006 by Elizabeth C. Gorski with the title: "Film Parade." Theme answers - scattered throughout the puzzle: TENTOMIDNIGHT NINEMONTHS EIGHTMENOUT SEVENSAMURAI SIXWEEKS FIVEEASYPIECES FOURSEASONS THREEKINGS TWOJAKES ONEHOURWITHYOU And then... COUNTDOWNIN TIMESSQUARE and HAPPYNEWYEAR Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/31/2006&g=15&d=D ....

If you haven't done a Liz Gorski puzzle in a while and miss her touch, the New Yorker has one with a bit of a bite (as Monday is their tough day): https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/04/13 Tough, but not that tough, at least from my perspective. Maybe the New Yorker is easing up on their puzzles, too. Or maybe I **am** getting better, all of a sudden!

Got sucked into crossword world about 2 months ago when my husband got me the NYT games subscription as a very early birthday present. This Monday solve was a personal best for me, and just so happened to fall on my actual birthday. Then I read the constructor notes - it must be fate!

Well I have been very much enjoying my time solving lately and that dash of coincidence pushed me to comment for the first time. 

Though as a woman, I can't say anything useful ever fits in my hip pocket!

I was drawn in to today's Wordplay Column by the adorable image!! Thank you for that :) The imagery that followed as I visualized a fellow cruciverbalist filling in the grid old-school style was also delightful, and comforting in the common humanity of it. I'm more familiar with Horray, so it took me a beat... fun Monday puzzle overall!
I looked to see if Ms. Spaight had any response to her indirect mention in the column. I don't see anything. I wonder if she is a little embarrassed or happy to be recognized.

I'm happy to inform everyone that they fixed the highlighting glitch in the app.

To see it displayed correctly, you must go to today's date in the Archive, long-press the number (the 13) and you will see a prompt to update the puzzle.

After that, it should display the themers correctly.

And we can all move on.

I am a few days late because I have been stewing on this, but I am here to whine about 29A in Friday's puzzle.  The only correct five-letter answer for "Like the glass in many a Dale Chihuly sculpture" is "BLOWN, not "FUSED."  Chihuly works are blown or hot sculpted, but not fused.  Fusing is a radically different process, involving a kiln, starting with cold glass, not hot glass, and simply does not produce work like what Chihuly makes.  For what it's worth, this answer not only annoyed me, but also frustrated a glass artist I know whose work appeared on the front page of the Times about a year ago, and who is certainly an expert in the field.

It's one thing to have a clue with two possible answers where the right one is obscure - I actually enjoy those because I can learn things from them and stretch my brain - and entirely another to have a clue that requires a wrong answer because it seems adjacent to the correct one to the constructor (and, apparently, the editor)  It was very disappointing.

I'm really only commenting on this because I found out last night from friends who also solve the crossword that this same error occurred in another puzzle in the last couple of years.  They were not any happier about seeing this clue/answer combination on Friday than I was.
Other letters one can make from an I are BDFJKMNPRUVXY.

Very fun Monday puzzle! I didn't get the revealer right away...I had HURRICANESEASON filled in first and couldn't figure out what one might say with "hurricane" to express joy. So I got to have the fun of watching the theme come together, which I sometimes fill the Monday in too fast to really notice. That's worth a cheer to me!

I always solve crosswords from the bottom up.  It's a bit different, but it works for me.  Couple that with the incorrect highlighting and my inattentiveness.  I had a very different idea for the theme.  I saw rumba, the beginning of which is rum.  Then I saw hurricane.   I thought for sure we were getting a certain mixed drink from the New Orleans area.  

I was very confused by the last two theme answers!

I misread 48D as "Features of lasagna or an ogre"
Felt surprised at a random Shrek reference.
Hip, Hip Hurricane Ray!

That owl’s name is “Little Hoot.”

I knew him briefly back in ‘86 while working a summer gig at the Maricopa County Fair in Arizona. He was operating the ferris wheel and I was working a booth on the midway. Every day we’d see each other at lunchtime in the staff area. Little Hoot would always order the same thing: three corn dogs with cheese sauce on the side for dipping, and a bag of bbq potato chips.  One day I showed up late and he was already sat at the picnic table, working on those dogs. By the time I sat down next to him with my own lunch, he was nearly finished. I said, “Hey Little Hoot, you chow down those corndogs any faster, I’ll have to call you ‘Corn Hoot!’”  Well, Little Hoot didn’t like that one bit. He looked up at me with those piercing eyes and just stared, shaking and frothing at the break. But like, REALLY intensely with the frothing. I didn’t know what to do so I just sat there looking back at him.  We sat like that for three hours, he and I. Just me looking at him, and him shaking and frothing and staring back at me. Later that night I learned that little Hoot got sloshed and died in a bar brawl out in Cave Creek.  We never really got to know one another that well, but he was an interesting bird, I’ll say that.

As a crossword fan I try to grid my LOINs, personally. Although I do recall that famous knight of the round table, Sir Loin, who was always girding his loins in the search for the Holy Grill.

@Francis
Thanks for the belly laugh, Francis!  I really needed a laugh today. Feeling totally ungirded now.

@Francis

Count this as one of the many expressions I never gave a second thought to… until now. Your ramblings about loins and girding made me wonder what it actually means to gird a loin. Is it painful? Is it reversible? Does it require special tools? 

Professor Google tells me that girding one’s loins meant strapping a belt around the waist and then tucking the long ends of the wearer’s tunic into the belt, leaving the legs free to run, unencumbered, into battle. 

I guess this means that once the battle is over, it’s safe to ungird, and the damage would likely be minimal. But what if you couldn’t find a belt in the first place? Would you trip over your loins? Would you experience gird envy?  Would you (gasp) be hoisted by your own petard?

Fortunately, we have better things to do with our modern day loins. Like teaching them how to hip hip HURRUMBA! 

(I am not going to let this go. 😁)

@john ezra -- Can't help you on your question re the number of words starting with A.  All I can tell you as that the letter A is typically 12% of the lettered squares in a puzzle, and that's right where it is today.  But that doesn't answer your question.  

But  may I add ... that your second paragraph, where you made a story out of the day's answers -- you are the master at this.  No one in the world is as good as you at this.  Always funny and witty and bringing peak entertainment.  I love when you do this, and you were right at the top of your game today.  Bravo, sir!

(I also have a wire haired fox terrier, Asta’s breed, but his delightful presence is unrelated to the movies.)

Seems like this had more of 'em than usual but guess not. And M. Biggen, ok ok I'll try the Thin Man flicks with a more open mindset! They might be just the thing to elevate my spirit these days.

@Mean Old Lady Here’s my take.

ADHERE refers to things physically sticking together (as with glue). COHERE refers to things figuratively sticking together (like people working as a team). Think also of the word “coherent”.

@Mean Old Lady

Quite literally, COHERE means “stick with.” Adhere means “stick to.”

@Mean Old Lady - In the world of science, ADHERE means to stick two different things together, whereas COHERE means like things sticking together. For example, water molecules are famously coherent, meaning they stick to each other, one result of which is surface tension. But they also adhere to polar things, one result of which is capillary action (which can also involve some adhesion.)

Ugh. My last word should have been "COhesion" not "ADhesion"...

@J.G. Harrington To an amateur, Chihuly's larger scale sculptures would seem to be blown glass fused together.

@J.G. Harrington
I immediately wrote in "blown."  When it finally unraveled  to FUSED, I tried to picture that, and couldn't.  Chihuly's beautiful work is blown.

@J.G. Harrington I believe that you are mistaken. I've spent a good amount of time in glass art studios, and most glass artworks involve both blowing and FUSing. If you look at these "flowers" by Chihuly, you should notice that they are composed of separate pieces that are FUSED together. https://modernartifact.com/collections/dale-chihuly-glass-flowers/products/dale-chihuly-massive-illuminated-commissioned-ikebana-garden-hand-blown-glass-sculpture-71-four-piece-300k There are some artists who only use fusing (like Toots Zynsky), and there are some who use completely different techniques (like pâte de verre or grinding or sand-blasting), but it is most common for glass artists to use both blowing and fusing.
@Tom Bantle An amateur never would have heard of fusing as a glass technique.

@C Diamond Maybe you haven't seen Shrek the Musical, but Shrek tells Donkey in it that ogres are like onions. Why? Because they smell bad? Because if you leave one out in the sun it gets weird hairs? No! Layers. Onions have layers, and ogres have layers. So you are 100% correct.
